svg.font-family: Comic Sans MS, Chalkboard, cursive, sans-serif
svg.text: From handshake to data theft — every step on the wire

g.text: Step 1: The Handshake — "Hello, let's talk securely!"
This is the LAST time firewall sees details!
What the firewall sees (and ONLY this):
Firewall can ONLY see: IP addresses, ports, and packet sizes. Nothing else!
Step 3: Connection ID Rotation — "Changing my name tag"
QUIC changes Connection IDs regularly for privacy (so nobody can track you)
New CID = can't link to old!
"I see packets with CID a1b2c3d4 stopped..."
"Now I see packets with CID e5f6a7b8..."
"Are they the same connection? I DON'T KNOW! The change was encrypted!"
Step 4: Server Migration — "Talk to me HERE instead"
The server tells the client (secretly, encrypted!) to migrate to a better address
This is the KEY to the attack:
The preferred_address is ENCRYPTED — firewall never sees it
Client migrating to a new IP is NORMAL in QUIC
So malware can send data to ANY IP and claim it's a "preferred address"!
Step 5: The Spy Watches — "Learning your patterns"
Malware on your PC silently records all outgoing QUIC traffic
"Now I know exactly how to make FAKE packets look REAL!"
The spy waits for a Connection ID to be RETIRED (this happens normally)
Then it hijacks the retired CID to start sending fake migration packets!
Step 6: THE ATTACK — "Stealing data disguised as migration"
What the FAKE packet looks like inside:
Contains: PATH_CHALLENGE + YOUR STOLEN DATA (passwords, files, keys...)
Firewall CANNOT see this part — all encrypted!
Stealth tricks used by the malware:
Packet size = 1200 bytes (same as real PATH_CHALLENGE packets)
Timing = copied from real traffic (50ms, 120ms, 30ms gaps)
DCID = reuses a retired CID (looks like a legitimate old connection)
Payload entropy = AES-256 encrypted (looks like normal QUIC encryption)
The stolen data keeps flowing out, packet after packet, completely undetected...
Step 7: Can YOU tell the difference?
Side by side: Real migration vs. Data theft. Spot the fake one!
They look IDENTICAL to the firewall!
Fake: dst is attacker on Google Cloud (34.102.x.x)
But both are Google IP ranges! WHOIS says "Google LLC" for both!
Firewall has NO WAY to know one is evil!
5 AI/ML detectors tried to tell them apart:
QUIC encrypts EVERYTHING after the handshake
Firewall is blind to content, CID changes, key rotations, preferred_address
IP address changes are NORMAL in QUIC (that's the whole feature!)
Firewall can't block migrations without breaking legitimate QUIC traffic
Malware copies EXACT packet sizes, timing, and entropy from real traffic
Even AI/ML classifiers can't tell fake packets from real ones (best F1: 0.35)
Evil server can be on Google Cloud / AWS (same WHOIS as real servers!)
IP reputation checks don't help — attacker uses same cloud as YouTube/Google
→ breaks privacy (defeats the point of QUIC!)
4. Check IPs against cloud provider ranges
→ attacker can use same cloud as real servers
Based on: "QUIC-Exfil: Exploiting QUIC's Server Preferred Address Feature" — ASIA CCS 2025
University of Zurich — Grübl, Niu, von der Assen, Stiller
